The **Brick Community Stadium** is a multi-use stadium in Robin Park in [Wigan](/wiki/Wigan "Wigan"), [Greater Manchester](/wiki/Greater_Manchester "Greater Manchester"), England. It is used by [Wigan Warriors](/wiki/Wigan_Warriors "Wigan Warriors") [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") club and [Wigan Athletic](/wiki/Wigan_Athletic_F.C. "Wigan Athletic F.C.") [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club. The stadium is owned by local businessman, Mike Danson, who owns both Wigan Athletic and the Wigan Warriors.

Built and opened in 1999,[[2]](#cite_note-jjbw-2) it was initially the **JJB Stadium** after its main sponsor.[[5]](#cite_note-5) In [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") matches, it was called **Wigan Athletic Stadium** due to UEFA regulations on sponsorship.[[6]](#cite_note-6) From 2009 to 2024, it was renamed the **DW Stadium**, and from 13 May 2024, it was again renamed, this time as the **Brick Community Stadium**, reflecting a partnership with a local charity.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The stadium was built by [Alfred McAlpine](/wiki/Alfred_McAlpine "Alfred McAlpine").[[8]](#cite_note-8) Wigan Athletic and Wigan Warriors moved into it from their long-term homes of [Springfield Park](/wiki/Springfield_Park_(Wigan) "Springfield Park (Wigan)") and [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park_(Wigan) "Central Park (Wigan)") respectively. International rugby league matches have also taken place at the venue.

Its current capacity is 25,138—seated in four single-tier stands—and its record attendance was on 11 May 2008 when 25,133 people watched Wigan Athletic play [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United "Manchester United") in the final match of the [2007–08 Premier League](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_Premier_League "2007–08 Premier League") season.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brick_Community_Stadium&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### JJB Stadium era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brick_Community_Stadium&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: JJB Stadium era")]

The stadium was built by [Alfred McAlpine](/wiki/Alfred_McAlpine "Alfred McAlpine") and completed in August 1999.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Wigan Athletic had spent the previous 67 years playing at [Springfield Park](/wiki/Springfield_Park_(Wigan) "Springfield Park (Wigan)"), and their first match at what was initially called the **JJB Stadium** was a friendly against [Morecambe](/wiki/Morecambe_F.C. "Morecambe F.C."), just before the stadium's official opening.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/JJB_Stadium_logo.png/250px-JJB_Stadium_logo.png)](/wiki/File:JJB_Stadium_logo.png)

The JJB Stadium logo (1999–2009)

The stadium's inauguration was marked with a friendly between Wigan Athletic and neighbours [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") – who were then reigning [European champions](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League"), [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") champions and [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") holders – with United's manager [Sir Alex Ferguson](/wiki/Alex_Ferguson "Alex Ferguson") officially opening the stadium.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The first competitive football match there took place on 7 August 1999, with Wigan Athletic facing [Scunthorpe United](/wiki/Scunthorpe_United_F.C. "Scunthorpe United F.C.") in a [Second Division](/wiki/Football_League_Second_Division "Football League Second Division") match. [Simon Haworth](/wiki/Simon_Haworth "Simon Haworth") scored twice, including the first competitive goal at the new stadium, as Athletic won 3–0.[[13]](#cite_note-DWRename-13)

[Wigan Warriors](/wiki/Wigan_Warriors "Wigan Warriors") moved to the stadium a month after it opened, once they had played their final home game of the 1999 regular season at [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park_(Wigan) "Central Park (Wigan)"), which had been the club's home since 1902. After their former ground was sold, the possibility of ground sharing with [Bolton Wanderers](/wiki/Bolton_Wanderers_F.C. "Bolton Wanderers F.C.") at the Reebok Stadium (now [University of Bolton Stadium](/wiki/University_of_Bolton_Stadium "University of Bolton Stadium")) was presented, but the new stadium in Wigan was chosen instead.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Their first game there was a play-off match against [Castleford Tigers](/wiki/Castleford_Tigers "Castleford Tigers"), which they lost, on 19 September.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The Warriors did not lose a competitive match at the stadium in 2001 and 2020.[[2]](#cite_note-jjbw-2)
[[16]](#cite_note-RLP-16)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/DW_Stadium.jpg/500px-DW_Stadium.jpg)](/wiki/File:DW_Stadium.jpg)

A view of the DW Stadium, from the bridge crossing the [Leeds and Liverpool Canal](/wiki/Leeds_and_Liverpool_Canal "Leeds and Liverpool Canal")

The first away team to win a competitive football match at the stadium was Wigan Athletic. A first round [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") tie against non-league [Cambridge City](/wiki/Cambridge_City_F.C. "Cambridge City F.C.") was played there due to City's ground being deemed unsuitable to host the tie. Wigan played in their changed strip and used the away dressing room since it was technically a 'home' game for Cambridge City. A [Stuart Barlow](/wiki/Stuart_Barlow "Stuart Barlow") brace secured the win for Wigan.[[13]](#cite_note-DWRename-13)

On 7 March 2005, [Greater Manchester](/wiki/Greater_Manchester "Greater Manchester") police announced that they would stop policing Wigan Athletic matches at the stadium from 2 April. This move would almost certainly have resulted in the stadium's safety certificate being revoked, effectively forcing the team to play behind closed doors. The move was part of an ongoing dispute between the police force and Dave Whelan surrounding £300,000 in unpaid policing costs. The police's decision would not have affected Wigan Warriors, whose games are stewarded instead of policed. The situation was temporarily resolved on 8 March with both sides reaching an agreement that would allow Athletic to play at the ground until the end of the season. Four months later, Wigan Athletic, facing the prospect of playing their home games in the Premier League in an empty stadium, grudgingly paid the money they owed to the police. The club successfully appealed against the payments in court and won damages from the police.[[17]](#cite_note-GMP-17)

On 7 September 2008, Wigan Warriors revealed plans to take their [Super League](/wiki/Super_League "Super League") Play-Off against [Bradford Bulls](/wiki/Bradford_Bulls "Bradford Bulls") to a neutral venue.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The controversial relocation was forced due to a fixture clash, with a match between football clubs Wigan Athletic and Sunderland to take place less than 24 hours after the Super League match.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-home_from_home-20) Whelan, who controlled Wigan Athletic, refused permission for the Warriors to stage their elimination at the stadium, citing concerns over the playing surface.[[21]](#cite_note-fananger-21) The game was relocated to [Widnes Vikings](/wiki/Widnes_Vikings "Widnes Vikings") home ground, the [Stobart Stadium](/wiki/Halton_Stadium "Halton Stadium").[[20]](#cite_note-home_from_home-20) In the same season, JJB Sports announced they would not continue to sponsor Wigan Warriors, leaving them without a main shirt sponsor.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

The stadium's average attendance has increased significantly since its opening in 1999. The Wigan Warriors' average attendance has increased by 32.5% from its first full season at the stadium in 2000, and Wigan Athletic's average attendance has increased by 181.2% from the 2000–01 season. The highest recorded attendance for a rugby league match is shared between three fixtures; the Wigan Warriors' fixture against [St Helens R.F.C.](/wiki/St_Helens_R.F.C. "St Helens R.F.C.") on 25 March 2005; Game 4 of the 2005 Tri-Nations series between [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain_national_rugby_league_team "Great Britain national rugby league team") and [Australia](/wiki/Australia_national_rugby_league_team "Australia national rugby league team") on 6 November; and Game 5 of the 2004 Tri-Nations series between Great Britain and Australia on 13 November at 25,004 each.[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-Tri-Nations2005-25)[[26]](#cite_note-Tri-Nations2004-26) The highest recorded football attendance at the stadium was Wigan Athletic's home fixture against Manchester United on 11 May 2008—the final day of the [2007–08 Premier League season](/wiki/Premier_League_2007%E2%80%9308 "Premier League 2007–08")—with 25,133 fans attending.[[27]](#cite_note-ManUtd2008-27) This is the stadium's highest recorded overall attendance to date, and was the match where Manchester United were crowned Premier League champions for that season.[[28]](#cite_note-UnitedPremier-28)

### DW Stadium era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brick_Community_Stadium&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: DW Stadium era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/4/4c/DW_Stadium_logo.svg/250px-DW_Stadium_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:DW_Stadium_logo.svg)

The DW Stadium logo 2009–2018

In March 2009, Dave Whelan acquired a chain of fitness clubs from [JJB Sports](/wiki/JJB_Sports "JJB Sports"). In the process, Whelan used the business to set up a new venture, DWSportsfitness and announced that the stadium name would change to the **DW Stadium** in August.[[29]](#cite_note-WiganDWRename-29) Whelan also announced that at the same time the stadium was renamed, its ownership would pass from himself to Wigan Athletic.[[30]](#cite_note-AthleticOwnership-30) Concerns about the future of Wigan Warriors were arrested in the same announcement, as Whelan extended the lease on the stadium by 50 years for the rugby league team.[[30]](#cite_note-AthleticOwnership-30) Before their match against [Leeds Rhinos](/wiki/Leeds_Rhinos "Leeds Rhinos") in July 2009, both clubs were given the opportunity to rename one stand, with the intention of renaming them in honour to a recognised player from each club's history. The rugby league club were granted the East Stand, which they renamed 'The Boston Stand' in tribute to the Welsh [wing](/wiki/Rugby_league_positions#Wing "Rugby league positions") [Billy Boston](/wiki/Billy_Boston "Billy Boston"),[[31]](#cite_note-StandRename-31) As Wigan Athletic had spent many years in the lower leagues it was recognised that most of their players were not known, so the West Stand was renamed 'The Springfield Stand' after the club's former ground.[[31]](#cite_note-StandRename-31)

In August 2020 it announced that DW Sports was to enter administration.[[32]](#cite_note-adminbbc-32) Later that month it was announced that [Frasers Group](/wiki/Frasers_Group "Frasers Group") would buy "certain" assets from DW Sports Fitness for £37m, but would not be using the firm's brand name.[[33]](#cite_note-bbcsd-33)

The match of 8 October 2022 against Cardiff City was controversial when it turned out one goal was bigger than the other. The match went ahead when officials ruled that there was no advantage to either team as they changed ends at half-time.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Cardiff won 3–1.

### The Brick Community Stadium era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brick_Community_Stadium&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: The Brick Community Stadium era")]

From 13 May 2024, the stadium was renamed **The Brick Community Stadium**, following a partnership with a local Wigan charity, The Brick, which works to address poverty and homelessness. The name will remain in place until the end of 2025; Wigan Athletic and Wigan Warriors committed to securing a long-term commercial stadium partner for 2026 and beyond.[[35]](#cite_note-WW-09May2024-35)

## Structure and facilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brick_Community_Stadium&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Structure and facilities")]

The stadium design is based on cantilevered, prefabricated steel roof and terrace structuring.[[2]](#cite_note-jjbw-2) It is an all-seater arena with a [seating capacity](/wiki/Seating_capacity "Seating capacity") of 25,138.[[2]](#cite_note-jjbw-2) The stands are rectangular and both the northern and southern stands have supporting steel girders suspended from beneath the roof. The four stands are of approximately the same height, however the stadium is not totally enclosed, leaving four exposed corners.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

The seats are a mixture of both resident teams' main colours of red and blue. The stadium is fully compliant with safety guidelines for a sports ground.[[37]](#cite_note-facilities-37)

The stadium also has facilities and access for up to 90 fans with disabilities, with facilities for partially sighted fans.[[37]](#cite_note-facilities-37)

The pitch is large enough to conform with both [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") and the standard rugby league requirements, at 6,110 by 5,460 metres (6,680 yd × 5,970 yd). This leaves an in-goal area just 5 metres (5.5 yd) deep for rugby matches. It is mostly made of natural grass, with 2% of the pitch composed of synthetics to provide stability.[[2]](#cite_note-jjbw-2) The ground has irrigation, and an under-heating system to resist icy weather.[[2]](#cite_note-jjbw-2)

### Boston Stand (east)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brick_Community_Stadium&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Boston Stand (east)")]

**Capacity – 8,238**  
The Boston Stand, named after Wigan Warriors legend [Billy Boston](/wiki/Billy_Boston "Billy Boston"), runs parallel to the western 'Springfield Stand' along the side of the pitch. The Boston Stand is the largest stand in the stadium, holding an electronic scoreboard and has WIGAN spelt out in the seats and has been occupied by the home fans since 2005.[[2]](#cite_note-jjbw-2)

### Springfield Stand (west)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brick_Community_Stadium&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Springfield Stand (west)")]

**Capacity – 6,100**  
The Springfield Stand, named after Wigan Athletic's former ground Springfield Park contains the stadium's vital facilities; four dressing rooms, benches, a doping control room and a treatment room for the players, as well as four executive boxes, ten radio commentary points and a designated TV studio.[[2]](#cite_note-jjbw-2)[[37]](#cite_note-facilities-37)

### Heatable South Stand (south)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brick_Community_Stadium&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Heatable South Stand (south)")]

**Capacity – 5,400**  
The South Stand was named the "Leam Richardson stand" in November 2021, after Wigan Athletic striker [Charlie Wyke](/wiki/Charlie_Wyke "Charlie Wyke") collapsed during training after suffering a cardiac arrest. Following his discharge from hospital Wyke praised Richardson's swift initiation of [CPR](/wiki/CPR "CPR") saying: "...*my life has been saved by the actions of the gaffer [Leam Richardson] and the club doctor Jonathan Tobin..."*.[[38]](#cite_note-38) It is used to seat the home fans behind the goal, opposite to the away fans in the North Stand.
In January 2024, the stand was renamed as the "Heatable South Stand", for the remainder of the 2023/24, and 2024/25 seasons, as part of a multi year deal.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

### North Stand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brick_Community_Stadium&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: North Stand")]

**Capacity- 5,400**  
At both Wigan Athletic and Wigan Warriors matches, away supporters are situated in the North Stand behind the goal.[[40]](#cite_note-worldstadia-40) Occasionally, during rugby games which attract low away support, the 5,400 capacity North Stand is closed altogether, and the away fans who attend are put into an alternative stand.[[2]](#cite_note-jjbw-2)[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Rising success on the pitch has been met with increased attendances. Promotion into the Premier League meant that in their first season of English top-flight [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"), Wigan Athletic's average home attendance almost doubled from the season before. Over three times more fans attended matches at the stadium during Wigan's 2007–08 season in the Premier League than had attended in the 2001–02 season when Wigan Athletic were in the Football League Second Division. Wigan Athletic's average home attendance for 2007–08 was the lowest out of all 20 teams in the Premier League, failing to make the top 30 English clubs in terms of attendance.[[45]](#cite_note-45) The same season saw the highest ever attendance at the stadium, when 25,133 people witnessed Wigan play [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") on the final day of the season.[[27]](#cite_note-ManUtd2008-27)

Wigan Athletic's average attendance was again the lowest in the league for the [2008–09 Premier League](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_Premier_League "2008–09 Premier League") season.[[46]](#cite_note-46) Premier League attendances fell on average by around 426 per club during the 2008–09 season. Wigan Athletic's home attendance fell by more than this, with their average attendance for the 2008–09 season falling by 633 from the season before.[[43]](#cite_note-LaticsAttendance-43) The highest attendance at the stadium for this season was a match between Wigan Athletic and Arsenal F.C., in which 22,954 people were counted. This attendance was 2,357 fans lower than the highest attendance in the season before.[[43]](#cite_note-LaticsAttendance-43)[[44]](#cite_note-LaticsHigh2009-44)

Attendances have generally risen for the Wigan Warriors since the start of the 2002 season, averaging around 14,000 over the three seasons from 2006 to 2009. Aside from Grand Finals, the largest Super League attendance was recorded at the stadium in 2005 when Wigan Warriors played their local rivals, [St Helens R.F.C.](/wiki/St_Helens_R.F.C. "St Helens R.F.C.")[[53]](#cite_note-warriors05-53)

This match is also the highest home attendance in the Wigan Warriors' history at the stadium. The twenty thousand mark has been broken 17 times since moving to the new stadium, 12 times against St Helens RLFC, once against local rivals [Warrington Wolves](/wiki/Warrington_Wolves "Warrington Wolves") in the opening round of the 2008 [Super League XIII](/wiki/Super_League_XIII "Super League XIII") season, and once in July 2009 against the [Leeds Rhinos](/wiki/Leeds_Rhinos "Leeds Rhinos") following a campaign advertising the game as the 'Big One'.[[16]](#cite_note-RLP-16)[[63]](#cite_note-BigOne-63) The 20,000 attendance mark has also been surpassed in World Club Challenge fixtures against NRL clubs St George-Illawarra in 2011, Cronulla Sharks in 2017, and Sydney Roosters in 2019. In 2010, the Warriors were officially the biggest supported team in the Super League.

## Other events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brick_Community_Stadium&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Other events")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Great_Britain_-_New_Zealand_rugby_league_test%2C_2007.jpg/250px-Great_Britain_-_New_Zealand_rugby_league_test%2C_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Great_Britain_-_New_Zealand_rugby_league_test,_2007.jpg)

The final home Test for [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain_national_rugby_league_team "Great Britain national rugby league team") against [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_rugby_league_team "New Zealand national rugby league team"), played at the stadium in 2007

As well as the fixtures for the two domestic teams, the stadium is a venue for international rugby league. Since the stadium's construction in 1999, it has been an ever-present venue whenever International series have been played in England.

Its first involvement came during the [2004 series](/wiki/2004_Rugby_League_Tri-Nations "2004 Rugby League Tri-Nations"), where the home [Great Britain and Ireland national team](/wiki/Great_Britain_national_rugby_league_team "Great Britain national rugby league team") defeated the [Australians](/wiki/Australian_national_rugby_league_team "Australian national rugby league team") 24–12, with [Terry Newton](/wiki/Terry_Newton "Terry Newton") and [Andy Farrell](/wiki/Andy_Farrell "Andy Farrell") both scoring in their home town of Wigan.[[26]](#cite_note-Tri-Nations2004-26) The venue was again selected for the [2005 series](/wiki/2005_Rugby_League_Tri-Nations "2005 Rugby League Tri-Nations"), and again the match was between Great Britain and Australia—this time the home team lost 6–20—with [Greater Manchester](/wiki/Greater_Manchester "Greater Manchester") born [Adrian Morley](/wiki/Adrian_Morley "Adrian Morley") scoring Great Britain's solitary try.[[25]](#cite_note-Tri-Nations2005-25) Both matches were complete sell-outs, each having attendances above 25,000. The match in 2004 was the third highest attendance of the series, coming behind a match at the [City of Manchester Stadium](/wiki/City_of_Manchester_Stadium "City of Manchester Stadium") between Great Britain and Australia, and the series final between the same two teams at [Elland Road](/wiki/Elland_Road "Elland Road").[[64]](#cite_note-64)

In addition to the Tri-Nations, the stadium has also played host to visiting nations during their European tours. Australia played Great Britain in front of a sell-out crowd during the [2001 Kangaroo tour](/wiki/2001_Kangaroo_tour "2001 Kangaroo tour"), with the home side losing 8–28.[[65]](#cite_note-65) Australia narrowly defeated Great Britain again in 2003 at the stadium, winning by a margin of four points during their [2003 European Tour](/wiki/2003_Kangaroo_tour "2003 Kangaroo tour").[[66]](#cite_note-66) [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_rugby_league_team "New Zealand national rugby league team") have also played at the stadium during their tours. In 2002, a try scored in his home town by [Martin Gleeson](/wiki/Martin_Gleeson_(rugby_league) "Martin Gleeson (rugby league)") helped Great Britain to defeat the 'Kiwis' 16–10.[[67]](#cite_note-67) The visitors lost again during their 2007 tour, this time 28–22 in a closely fought game in which Wigan-born [second-row](/wiki/Rugby_league_positions#Second-row_forward "Rugby league positions") [Sean O'Loughlin](/wiki/Sean_O%27Loughlin "Sean O'Loughlin") featured.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

The stadium has hosted the [World Club Challenge](/wiki/World_Club_Challenge "World Club Challenge") four times, in [2000](/wiki/2000_World_Club_Challenge "2000 World Club Challenge"), between [St. Helens](/wiki/St_Helens_R.F.C. "St Helens R.F.C.") and the [Melbourne Storm](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm") in [2011](/wiki/2011_World_Club_Challenge "2011 World Club Challenge") when [Wigan](/wiki/Wigan_Warriors "Wigan Warriors") took on [St. George Illawarra Dragons](/wiki/St._George_Illawarra_Dragons "St. George Illawarra Dragons").[[69]](#cite_note-69) and in 2015 and 2016 when Wigan Warriors hosted the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos").

In 2025, the stadium hosted the [Women's Super League](/wiki/RFL_Women%27s_Super_League "RFL Women's Super League") Grand Final between [Wigan Warriors](/wiki/Wigan_Warriors "Wigan Warriors") and [St Helens](/wiki/St_Helens_R.F.C._Women "St Helens R.F.C. Women").[[70]](#cite_note-70)

## Surroundings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brick_Community_Stadium&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Surroundings")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Wigan_skyline_2008.jpg/250px-Wigan_skyline_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wigan_skyline_2008.jpg)

The Wigan skyline, featuring the stadium

The stadium's surroundings are mostly urban, as it is located in the north of Wigan's Robin Park retail complex in the western suburb of Newtown, on the south bank of the [River Douglas](/wiki/River_Douglas,_Lancashire "River Douglas, Lancashire"), west of the Miry Lane industrial estate. The stadium's car parks are situated around the canal, and can hold up to 2,500 cars.[[2]](#cite_note-jjbw-2)

The main road serving the complex is the [A49](/wiki/A577_road_(Great_Britain) "A577 road (Great Britain)"), running east-bound from the M6 motorway, junction 26, 750 metres (820 yd) south of the stadium.[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73) Both of the Wigan railway stations, [Wigan Wallgate](/wiki/Wigan_Wallgate_railway_station "Wigan Wallgate railway station") and [Wigan North Western](/wiki/Wigan_North_Western_railway_station "Wigan North Western railway station") lie 1.3–1.6 kilometres (0.8–1.0 mi) east of the stadium.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

Next to the stadium's South Stand lies the **Robin Park Arena**,[[75]](#cite_note-Robin_Park_locations-75) which is operated by Wigan Warriors and is capable of seating around 1,200 spectators.[[76]](#cite_note-76) The arena is mainly used as the indoor and outdoor training ground for Wigan Warriors and also [athletics](/wiki/Sport_of_athletics "Sport of athletics"). It also houses Wigan Warriors club shop, selling merchandise. It was formerly used as functions for: [North West Counties Football League](/wiki/North_West_Counties_Football_League "North West Counties Football League") side [Wigan Robin Park](/wiki/Wigan_Robin_Park "Wigan Robin Park"),[[77]](#cite_note-77) and Wigan Athletic Reserves. Wigan Warriors' former training base was at [Edge Hall Road](/wiki/Edge_Hall_Road "Edge Hall Road"). Robin Park Sports Centre is situated directly opposite the Stadium and Arena.[[75]](#cite_note-Robin_Park_locations-75)
